answer:
**Hindu Dharma texts (Smṛtis)** prominently engage with *mleccha* (often rendered as "barbarian" or "outsider") as foreigners, speakers of impure languages, inhabitants of lands beyond Āryāvarta, or those outside the *varṇa* system, frequently in contexts of purity, law, geography, and punishment.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5]

- *Manusmṛti*: Defines *mleccha*-lands beyond the black antelope's range; equates non-*varṇa* groups (even if speaking Aryan languages) with barbarians; discusses "outsiders" as defiled castes below Shudras.[^6][^7][^8]
- *Viṣṇu Smṛti*: Prohibits ancestral rites (*śrāddha*) in *mlecchadeśa* (barbarian lands); restricts contact and travel there.[^9]
- *Kātyāyana Smṛti*: No fines for beating *mlecchas* (barbarians), alongside untouchables and sinners.[^10]
- *Nāradasmṛti*: Outsiders as witnesses only among their own.[^11]

**Urdu/Persian poetry** (Islamic context) engages *kāfir* ("infidel," non-believer) theologically or satirically, questioning justice, philosophy, or prophecy.

- *Bang-E-Dara* (Allama Iqbal, Parts 1 & 3): Critiques views calling Hindus non-*kāfirs*; posits paradise rewards for *kafirs* following Muslim law.[^12][^13]
- *Diwan-E-Zauq* (Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq): Fears an "infidel" claiming prophethood via love's "seal."[^14]

No other works in the corpus substantially engage these figures; French texts use "barbarian" historically/ethnographically without the religious/caste connotations.[^15]

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 2, [p-265a9e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-265a9e).
[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 10, [p-19acb7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-19acb7).
[^3]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Ch. 84, [p-e5a586](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/84/translation/#p-e5a586).
[^4]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 66, [p-6be7c8](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/66/translation/#p-6be7c8).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 10, [p-550c60](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-550c60).
[^6]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 2, [p-265a9e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-265a9e).
[^7]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 10, [p-19acb7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-19acb7).
[^8]: *Manusmṛti*, Ch. 10, [p-04bffd](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/10/translation/#p-04bffd).
[^9]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, Ch. 84, [p-e5a586](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/84/translation/#p-e5a586).
[^10]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 66, [p-6be7c8](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/66/translation/#p-6be7c8).
[^11]: *Nāradasmṛti*, Ch. 1, [p-68eb5f](/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-68eb5f).
[^12]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 44, [p-266324](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/44-zuhd-aur-rindi/translation/#p-266324).
[^13]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 4, [p-072edd](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-072edd).
[^14]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, Ch. 59, [p-bc68e3](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/59-koi-in-tang-dahanon-se-mohabbat-na-kare/translation/#p-bc68e3).
[^15]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I* (Charles Dunoyer), various chapters.
